The production of extracellular polysaccharides, such as dextran and levan by bacteria is crucial for their ability to form biofilms on tooth surfaces. This allows them to persist despite mechanical cleaning efforts and creates an environment that supports further colonization by other cariogenic species. Additionally, these exopolysaccharides can serve as energy sources during periods of nutrient scarcity within the oral cavity.
